title: Hedonic Games, Stable Matchings, and House Allocation

charts.title: Week 5 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics in Week 5.

nodes.label:
Week 5
Cooperative Games Part II
nodes.note: Week 5 covers non-transferable utility settings: hedonic games where payoff is determined solely by coalition membership, stable matchings between two groups, and house allocation algorithms including TTC.

nodes.label:
Beyond TU:
NTU Games
nodes.note: In NTU games, payoffs cannot be freely redistributed. Hedonic games are a special case where each player's utility depends only on which coalition they belong to, with no action choice within a coalition.

nodes.label:
Hedonic
Games
nodes.note: A hedonic game has players with preferences over all coalitions they could belong to. The outcome is a partition of players into coalitions. No payoff distribution phase is needed.

nodes.label:
Compact
Representations
nodes.note: Full hedonic preferences are exponential. Compact forms include: additively separable hedonic games (ASHG, utility = sum over partners), fractional games (average), and friends-and-enemies games.

nodes.label:
Stability in
Hedonic Games
nodes.note: Multiple stability concepts form a hierarchy from strongest to weakest: core stable, Nash stable, individually stable, contractual individually stable (CIS), and individually rational (IR). CIS and IR always exist; stronger concepts may not.

nodes.label:
Stable
Matching
nodes.note: The stable matching problem matches two disjoint groups (e.g., men and women) with strict preferences. A matching is stable if no unmatched pair both prefer each other over their current partners (no blocking pair).

nodes.label:
Gale-Shapley
Algorithm
nodes.note: The Gale-Shapley deferred acceptance algorithm (1962) always finds a stable matching in O(n^2) rounds. When men propose, it produces the man-optimal (and women-pessimal) stable matching.

nodes.label:
Strategyproofness
of GS
nodes.note: GS is strategyproof for the proposing side (men already get their best valid partner; no misreport helps). It is NOT strategyproof for the receiving side (women can benefit from misreporting).

nodes.label:
House
Allocation
nodes.note: House allocation matches agents to houses in a one-sided market. Serial Dictatorship (strategyproof, PO, core stable) works without endowments. Top Trading Cycles (TTC) handles endowments and is also strategyproof, IR, PO, and in the core.

nodes.label:
Blocking Pairs
& Valid Partners
nodes.note: A blocking pair in a matching is a man-woman pair who both prefer each other to their current partners. An agent's valid partners are those matched to them in some stable matching.

nodes.label:
TTC Algorithm
& Properties
nodes.note: TTC: each agent points to their favourite house; each house points to its owner. Extract and allocate cycles; repeat. TTC is individually rational, Pareto-optimal, core stable, and strategyproof.

nodes.label:
Serial
Dictatorship
nodes.note: SD: agents choose in a fixed order, each picking their favourite remaining house. SD is strategyproof, PO, and core stable, but not fair (first agent gets top choice).

charts.title: Gale-Shapley Deferred Acceptance Algorithm
charts.description: Men-proposing DA algorithm that finds the man-optimal stable matching.

nodes.label:
Start: n men, n women
with strict preferences
nodes.note: Each man has a strict ranking of all women; each woman has a strict ranking of all men. Being unmatched is worst for all. We want to find a stable matching.

nodes.label:
All men unmatched;
all proposals lists full
nodes.note: Initialise: every man is unmatched and has a full list of women to propose to in decreasing preference order.

nodes.label:
Any unmatched man
with women left to propose?
nodes.note: Check if any man remains unmatched and has not yet proposed to all women. If yes, continue the algorithm. If no unmatched man remains (or all have exhausted their lists), we have a matching.

nodes.label:
Man m proposes to
his most preferred
unproposed woman w
nodes.note: The man who is unmatched proposes to the highest-ranked woman he has not yet proposed to. He is then removed from her to-do list.

nodes.label:
Does w currently
have a tentative partner?
nodes.note: Check whether the woman w who received the proposal is already tentatively matched to someone else, or if she is currently unmatched.

nodes.label:
Does w prefer m
over current partner m'?
nodes.note: Women only upgrade: w compares new proposer m to her current tentative partner m'. If w prefers m, she switches; otherwise she rejects m.

nodes.label:
w tentatively
accepts m; m' becomes free
nodes.note: Woman w drops her current partner m' (who becomes unmatched again) and tentatively accepts m. A woman can only get better or stay the same throughout the algorithm.

nodes.label:
w rejects m;
m remains unmatched
nodes.note: Woman w prefers her current tentative partner, so she rejects m. Man m stays unmatched and will propose to his next preferred woman in the next round.

nodes.label:
w tentatively
accepts m (no current partner)
nodes.note: Woman w is currently unmatched, so she tentatively accepts any proposal. She can still be replaced by a better proposer later.

nodes.label:
Man-optimal
stable matching found
nodes.note: Every man is matched to his best valid partner (man-optimal). Every woman is matched to her worst valid partner (women-pessimal). The matching is guaranteed stable and produced in at most n^2 rounds.

charts.title: Top Trading Cycles (TTC) Algorithm
charts.description: Algorithm for house allocation with endowments that is IR, PO, core stable, and strategyproof.

nodes.label:
Start: agents own houses;
each has preference ranking
nodes.note: Each agent owns one house and has strict preferences over all houses. We want a reallocation that respects individual rationality (no one ends up worse than their own house).

nodes.label:
Each agent points to
most preferred available house
nodes.note: In the current graph, every agent draws an edge to the house they like most among remaining (unallocated) houses. Every house draws an edge back to its current owner.

nodes.label:
Find a cycle
in the pointing graph
nodes.note: The graph has 2n vertices (n agents + n houses) and each has exactly one outgoing edge, so at least one cycle must exist. Find any such cycle.

nodes.label:
Allocate: each agent
in cycle gets pointed-to house
nodes.note: All agents in the cycle receive the house they are pointing to. This is their most preferred house from those remaining, so they are at least as happy as keeping their own house.

nodes.label:
Remove cycle agents
and houses from graph
nodes.note: Remove the matched agents and houses from the market. The remaining agents form a smaller subproblem with the same structure.

nodes.label:
Any unmatched
agents remaining?
nodes.note: Check if all agents have been allocated a house. Since each cycle removes at least one agent-house pair, the algorithm terminates in at most n iterations.

nodes.label:
TTC allocation
complete
nodes.note: Every agent has been matched to a house. The resulting allocation is individually rational (no one gets a house they prefer less than their own), Pareto-optimal, in the core, and the mechanism is strategyproof.

charts.title: Stability Hierarchy in Hedonic Games
charts.description: Chain of stability concepts from strongest (core stable) to weakest (individually rational), with existence guarantees.

nodes.label:
Hedonic game:
find stable partition
nodes.note: Given a hedonic game, we want to find a partition of players into coalitions satisfying a stability concept. Stronger concepts rule out more deviations but may not always exist.

nodes.label:
Core Stable:
no group can all
benefit by deviating
nodes.note: A partition is core stable if no group of players all prefer forming a coalition together over their current coalitions. This is the strongest stability concept but may not exist.

nodes.label:
Core stable
outcome exists?
nodes.note: Check whether any partition is core stable. The core can be empty even in symmetric additively separable hedonic games.

nodes.label:
Nash Stable:
no single player
wants to move
nodes.note: A partition is Nash stable if no individual player prefers moving to another coalition (or being alone) over their current position. Nash stability implies individual rationality.

nodes.label:
Nash stable
outcome exists?
nodes.note: Nash stable outcomes exist for symmetric ASHGs (every NS-deviation increases social welfare, so improvement dynamics converge). May not exist in general hedonic games.

nodes.label:
CIS: no player
benefits with consent
of all involved
nodes.note: Contractual individual stability (CIS): a deviation requires the deviating player, the receiving coalition, and the leaving coalition to all weakly approve. CIS outcomes always exist.

nodes.label:
Individually Rational:
no one prefers
being alone
nodes.note: The weakest stability: every player weakly prefers their current coalition to being a singleton. IR outcomes always exist (e.g., the all-singletons partition satisfies IR trivially when singletons have zero utility).

nodes.label:
No stable outcome
exists at this level
nodes.note: The chosen stability concept is too strong for this game instance. The empty core or absence of Nash stable outcomes means we must fall back to a weaker concept.
